digest.intro: War over a waterway, open-source AI from China, and audience capture cracking.

items.title: The War Is Now Explicitly About the Strait of Hormuz
items.blurb: US forces launched a second wave of strikes Saturday after Iranian ballistic missiles and drones killed American service members in Jordan; Iran responded by suspending the Islamabad MoU, declaring the US in violation. The EU, Gulf bloc, and others have already rejected US sovereignty claims over the strait — meaning Washington is now fighting for a waterway that the rest of the world refuses to concede.

items.title: Libya Joins China's Payment System — Quietly
items.blurb: While the US fights to control Hormuz traffic, Libya connecting to China's settlement infrastructure is the slower, structural story running in parallel — the financial rewiring of the Middle East and Africa proceeds regardless of who controls the shipping lanes. Dollar erosion isn't a dramatic event; it's this kind of incremental replumbing.

items.title: Kimi K3: China Drops a 2.8 Trillion Parameter Open Model
items.blurb: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 is the first open-weight model at the 3T-class scale — built on two new architectural ideas, Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals, with a 1-million-token context window. It trails Claude Fable 5 and GPT 5.6 Sol overall but benchmarks at frontier level on coding, and full weights drop July 27. Timed directly before Xi's WAIC speech, this is as much a geopolitical statement as a research release.

items.title: AI Mania Is Eviscerating Organizational Judgment
items.blurb: After 300+ conversations with professionals from frontline workers to Fortune 500 executives, Nik Suresh describes a specific mass dysfunction: people in charge either have no plan or see no path except keeping their heads down while 'frothing excitement' captures institutions. The piece is sharp because it names the social mechanics — the saner people are silent not because they're wrong, but because their wellbeing depends on not saying so.

items.title: The Looting of Science Fiction
items.blurb: Ali Rıza Taşkale's piece (surfaced via Sentiers) maps how Silicon Valley founders systematically borrow SF's imagery while discarding its warnings: Musk invokes Star Trek, Zuckerberg renamed his company after Snow Crash's dystopia, Palantir takes its name from Tolkien's all-seeing surveillance stone. The Torment Nexus pattern isn't accidental — the caution is read as a product brief. Sentiers pairs it with a short history of techno-negativity and a sharp take on LLMs at work before going on summer break until August 23.

items.title: What I Learned Selling 2,500 MIDI Recorders
items.blurb: Weinberger's core finding after taking Jamcorder from idea to 2,500 units: the hardware wasn't the hard part — 200,000 lines of firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling across three years (pre-LLM) was. He argues hardware's reputation for difficulty is systematically overstated, and that the saying 'hardware is hard' functions more as a social filter than an accurate description. The kind of obsessive single-thing account that recalibrates how you think about what's actually blocking you.

items.title: The Scariest Chart in Electrical Engineering
items.blurb: Veritasium does what he does best: takes a deeply counterintuitive visual artifact — the Smith Chart, which compresses complex RF impedance math onto a single circle — and reveals why engineers use it constantly without fully understanding what it's actually representing geometrically. Worth watching just for the moment the circular mapping clicks.

facts.fact: For nine of the past twelve months, Kimi models have held the record for largest open-weight model size — making Moonshot AI the sustained leader at the open-source scaling frontier, not a one-time entrant.

Medieval France's pointy shoe fad (poulaines) got so extreme that King Charles V passed a law banning their fabrication and use in Paris — an early recorded instance of legislating status symbols out of existence.

Palantir named itself after the all-seeing surveillance stones in Tolkien — a fact that reads less like a brand choice and more like a confession once you know what palantíri actually do in the books.

Chip Weinberger's MIDI recorder required over 200,000 lines of code across firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling — written largely before LLMs — versus hardware that went to 2,500 units with no scrapped production runs.

The EU and the Gulf bloc have both formally rejected US claims of sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz — meaning Washington is now in military conflict over a waterway the international community refuses to recognize as under American jurisdiction.

art_picks.title: Sans Soleil
art_picks.creator: Chris Marker
art_picks.why: The film that basically invented the essay-film as a form — Marker drifts between Tokyo, Guinea-Bissau, and Iceland narrating memory, ritual, and how images construct reality rather than record it. The passage on vertigo and false memory alone will make you want to build something. Pure rabbit hole.

art_picks.title: Cybernetic Serendipity: The Computer and the Arts (1968)
art_picks.creator: Jasia Reichardt / ICA London
art_picks.why: The exhibition that first showed the world generative, algorithmic, and computer-made art as a legitimate art scene — before the vocabulary existed. Studying the original catalog is less art history and more a blueprint for exactly the intersection of expressive systems and playful surfaces Willem works in. The ICA documentation is the closest living record.

art_picks.title: Hecker / Roden — Sand [2000, 2015 remaster]
art_picks.creator: Florian Hecker & Steve Roden
art_picks.why: Two artists who went unreasonably deep on a narrow thing: Hecker on psychoacoustic synthesis, Roden on found-sound and 'small sound' composition. The collaboration sounds like watching something geological happen in accelerated time — deep structure made audible, beautiful and strange. Perfect for a long making session.

events.name: Whitney Biennial 2026
events.venue: Whitney Museum of American Art, 99 Gansevoort St, Meatpacking District
events.why: This edition — organized around 'relationality' with 56 artists, heavy on millennials and interspecies/technological entanglements — is closing in five weeks; the kind of show that maps the actual current psychic terrain of American contemporary art before it hardens into retrospective.

events.name: Pierre Huyghe: UUmwelt
events.venue: MoMA Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden, 11 West 53rd St, Manhattan
events.why: Freestanding screens in MoMA's sculpture garden where AI trained on brain scans generates imagery activated in real time by visitor gaze — the literal edge where human cognition, machine cognition, and public space collapse into one another.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night: Carlos Souffront + Wackies Showcase
events.venue: MoMA PS1 Courtyard, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: Season opener features Lloyd 'Bullwackie' Barnes (founder of the Bronx's legendary reggae label Wackies) and Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus in a rare live collaboration — a set about dub's deep lineage — in the same courtyard that's hosted underground and avant-garde music since 1998.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — July 31: BAE BAE b2b Crystallmess + Gabber Eleganza
events.venue: MoMA PS1 Courtyard, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: Gabber Eleganza has built an entire subculture-as-mythology project around Italian rave history and queer club identity — exactly the kind of frontier scene made visible before it institutionalizes; the second Warm Up date has the most interesting cultural layer underneath it.

events.name: Greater New York 2026 + Precious Okoyomon Courtyard Commission
events.venue: MoMA PS1, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: PS1's survey of 50+ NYC-based artists in the early stages of their careers — the show that historically maps the emerging scene before it scatters — opens alongside Okoyomon's inaugural courtyard commission transforming the museum's outdoor space.

trial_sources.name: The Honest Broker — Ted Gioia
trial_sources.why: A prolific, deeply opinionated culture critic (Stanford, 12 books) who maps taste formation, cultural transmission, and the aesthetics of media with the same rigor the reader brings to geopolitics — widely cited by other good writers as the sharpest cultural signal-reader on Substack.

trial_sources.name: The Convivial Society — L. M. Sacasas
trial_sources.why: Called 'the public philosopher of technology' and recommended by Ezra Klein, Sacasas writes slow, precise essays on how communication tools reshape behavior, identity, and moral life — a direct match for the reader's interest in the aesthetics of technology and social software history.

trial_sources.name: Scott Carney's Substack
trial_sources.why: An investigative journalist exposing the line between credible regenerative medicine and grift — covering stem cells, peptides, and exosomes with scientific rigor and named sources, including interviews with leading biogerontologists like Matt Kaeberlein.
